request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"yo::social_communication","name":"Yo","one_liner":"Single-word push-notification social app that let users send only the word 'Yo' to friends, achieving viral fame before fading into irrelevance by 2016.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both are free B2C consumer social apps targeting viral, low-friction engagement. However, Yo's documented monetization was tiered_freemium (with no meaningful revenue layer ever activated), while the new pitch is explicitly ad-supported. The monetization architecture differs substantially even though both are free-to-download consumer apps."},"market":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both target consumer social communication on mobile, leveraging virality within constrained social groups. Yo's viral wave started within a specific employee network and spread via Product Hunt, paralleling the pitch's college-tower viral loop. Both operate in the hyper-niche, single-feature social app segment."},"gtm":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both rely on organic word-of-mouth and network-effect virality within tight communities rather than paid acquisition. Yo spread via Product Hunt and Mobli employees; the new pitch spreads through college dormitory towers. The go-to-market is community-seeded virality in both cases, though the new pitch targets a more geographically constrained seed audience."},"stage_scale":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both are early-stage B2C mobile apps with initial viral spikes and no demonstrated sustainable monetization at launch. Yo raised ~$2.5M at a $5-10M valuation on hype alone, suggesting comparable early traction-before-revenue dynamics to what the new pitch describes."}},"why_similar":"Both Yo and the new pitch are B2C consumer social mobile apps built on a single, radically constrained interaction mechanic (one word vs. one 30-second voice note from one location). Both rely entirely on organic viral growth within a tight seed community rather than paid GTM. Both are free products with no proven revenue engine at launch, and both court the 'simplicity as a feature' thesis in the hyper-competitive social app market.","where_diverged":"The new pitch diverges from Yo on at least three concrete dimensions: (1) Monetization model — the pitch is explicitly ad-supported (ad revenue), whereas Yo's documented monetization was tiered_freemium with no meaningful ad layer ever built; (2) Content format — the new pitch centers on 30-second audio voice notes, adding a richer media dimension absent from Yo's single-word/ping mechanic; (3) Geographic/contextual constraint — the pitch anchors posts to a single recurring physical location (a college tower), creating a place-based community loop that Yo never attempted. These differences could give the new pitch a stronger retention hook and a clearer ad-inventory story, though they also add product and moderation complexity Yo never faced.","failure_causes":["no sustainable monetization model activated","virality decayed without a retention loop","feature set too thin to defend against competitors and clones","product ran on autopilot after team attrition","failure to expand from novelty to genuine utility","security vulnerabilities eroded early user trust","over-reliance on hype and press cycle rather than organic DAU growth"],"lessons_for_input":["Define and activate your ad monetization layer before the viral spike fades — Yo never converted hype into revenue and shut down on empty coffers.","Build a retention loop beyond the novelty mechanic; the location-anchored voice note is interesting but must give users a reason to return daily after week one.","Lock in the community seed (the college tower) before scaling — Yo lost its tight-knit feel the moment it went mass-market, destroying the social graph density that made it fun.","Harden security early; Yo's phone-number exploit in its first weeks caused lasting trust damage — audio posts tied to physical locations raise even higher privacy stakes.","Plan for feature expansion that deepens the core mechanic rather than diluting it; Yo's add-ons (photos, location, groups) felt bolted on and did not arrest churn."]}
